title: Adequate Light Levels, Glare, and General Lighting Requirements
narrationScript: Here is an uncomfortable fact. Most missed indications are not failures of eyesight. They are failures of lighting. The crack was there, the inspector's vision was fine, and the light never gave the eye a fair chance. This lesson is about giving the eye that fair chance. How much light you need, how to prove you have it, and how glare and fatigue quietly take it away. Start with the numbers. Common codes and written procedures set a minimum of about five hundred lux, roughly fifty footcandles, for general visual examination, and about one thousand lux, one hundred footcandles, for critical or direct examination of a specific surface. One footcandle is about ten point seven six lux, so the conversion is easy. Footcandles times ten, plus a little. Those minimums are not bureaucracy. The odds of detecting a small, low-contrast indication climb steeply with illuminance, right through the range where dim shop corners and shadowed nozzle bores live. And the eye ages. An inspector in his fifties may need roughly twice the light he needed in his twenties to see the same detail, because the lens yellows and the pupil shrinks. The minimum is written for the whole crew, not just the youngest eyes. Now, proving it. A number in a procedure means nothing until you verify it with a calibrated light meter at the surface you are examining. Not at your chest. Not in the air. The sensor lies in the plane of the surface, right where the indication would be. Use the actual lamp you will inspect with, at the actual working distance, in the darkest, least accessible spot you must examine. Light follows the inverse square law. Double the distance from lamp to surface and illuminance drops to one quarter. A flashlight that floods the surface at twelve inches may fail the minimum at arm's length. Record the reading, the meter's serial number, and its calibration due date. And if your light runs on batteries, check it again as the shift wears on, because output sags as the batteries drain. More light is not automatically better, and glare is the reason. Direct glare is a bright source sitting in your field of view, like a floodlight behind the weld you are examining. Your pupils close down for the source, and the surface you care about goes dark. Reflected glare is the beam bouncing off a shiny surface straight into your eye, and it follows simple mirror geometry. The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence. If your lamp, the surface, and your eye line up at that mirror angle, you get a blinding hot spot. Veiling glare is the quiet one. Stray bright light scatters inside your own eye and spreads a wash of brightness across the retina, lowering the contrast of everything you look at. You do not have to feel dazzled for glare to be costing you indications. And because scatter inside the eye increases with age, veiling glare hits experienced inspectors hardest. The fixes are positional, not electrical. Move the lamp out of the mirror angle, or move your head. Shield the source so it is not in your field of view. Put a diffuser on a harsh point source. Tilt the part if you can. A few seconds of repositioning buys back more contrast than another hundred watts ever will. Glare also drives fatigue, and fatigue drives miss rates. Visual fatigue shows up as stinging eyes, headache, blurred focus, and a detection rate that quietly falls off near the end of a shift. Flicker from old fluorescent ballasts or cheaply dimmed LEDs adds to it. So does working hunched over a poorly lit surface, and so does walking in and out of a dark vessel all day, forcing your eyes to re-adapt every trip. Take short visual breaks on long examinations, rotate demanding inspection tasks when you can, and treat persistent eyestrain like a bad instrument. Find the cause and fix it. Put it together and general lighting requirements come down to a short list. Know the minimum your procedure requires. Provide it with ambient light plus a task light, not one or the other. Verify it with a calibrated meter at the surface, and write it down. Position the lighting to kill glare. Keep the direct viewing geometry in mind, eye within twenty-four inches of the surface, viewing angle no flatter than thirty degrees. And mind safety. Low-voltage or intrinsically safe lights inside vessels, ground-fault protection on cords, hot lamps away from solvents. Light is a tool. Set it up like one.

sections.heading: How Much Light Is Enough?
sections.body:
Detection needs light — a specific, verifiable amount of it. Codes and written procedures set minimum illuminance at the examination surface, and the most common figures in industry practice are about 500 lux (50 footcandles) for general visual examination and about 1000 lux (100 footcandles) for critical or direct examination — the values used, for example, in widely applied pressure-vessel code practice. Your governing document rules: read the procedure and use its numbers.

Why minimums at all? The probability of detecting a small, low-contrast indication climbs steeply with illuminance through exactly the range where shaded corners, bores, and nozzle interiors live. Below the minimum, you are gambling. The requirement also protects against more than dim corners — eyes age. Lens yellowing and a smaller resting pupil mean an inspector in his fifties may need roughly twice the illuminance he needed at twenty-five to resolve the same detail. The minimum is written for the whole workforce, not the youngest eyes on the crew. And more light helps only up to the point where glare and washout take over — which is why the answer to every lighting problem is not simply a bigger lamp.

sections.heading: Proving It: Meter at the Surface
sections.body:
A lighting requirement is meaningless until you verify it where it counts. The instrument is a calibrated light meter — calibration current, serial number recorded. The technique has four non-negotiables.

- **At the surface.** The sensor lies in the plane of the examination surface, at the actual spot to be examined — not at your chest, not in mid-air facing the lamp.
- **With the working lamp.** Verify the actual flashlight or fixture you will inspect with, at the actual working distance and aim. Catalog lumens prove nothing about lux on a weld toe.
- **At the worst location.** Check the darkest, least accessible area in the examination scope — the bore, the far fillet, the underside. If the worst spot passes, the rest will.
- **On the record.** Note the reading, the meter ID and calibration due date, and the lamp used. If the job runs long on battery lamps, re-check as output sags with battery drain.

Distance dominates: illuminance from a small source falls with the square of distance, so a flashlight that floods a surface at 12 inches may fail the minimum at arm's length. Verify at the distance you will actually hold it.

sections.caption: Illuminance is verified with a calibrated meter lying at the examination surface, using the working lamp at working distance — alongside the direct-viewing geometry limits.

sections.title: The Numbers Procedures Use
sections.body:
- General visual examination: at least ~500 lx (50 fc) at the surface.
- Critical / direct examination: at least ~1000 lx (100 fc) at the surface.
- Conversion: 1 fc ≈ 10.76 lx.
- Direct-viewing geometry: eye within 600 mm (24 in.) of the surface, viewing angle not less than 30° to the surface.
- Verification: calibrated light meter at the examination surface; record reading, meter ID, and calibration due date.

Always defer to the specific procedure and code edition governing your job.

sections.heading: Glare: Where Contrast Goes to Die
sections.body:
Glare is unwanted brightness in the wrong place, and it costs you indications three ways.

**Direct glare** is a bright source in your field of view — a floodlight behind the part, a sunlit doorway beyond the weld. Your pupil constricts for the source, not for the surface, and everything you came to see darkens.

**Reflected (specular) glare** is the beam bouncing off a glossy surface into your eye. It obeys mirror geometry: the angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence. Put your eye at that mirror angle on a machined flange or a wet surface and you get a blinding hot spot exactly where you are trying to look.

**Veiling glare** is the quiet one. Stray bright light scatters inside the eye itself and lays a luminous haze over the retinal image, cutting the contrast of everything — you need not feel dazzled to be losing detection capability. Scatter inside the eye increases with age, so veiling glare hits experienced inspectors hardest.

The fixes are geometric, not electrical: move the lamp out of the mirror angle, or move your head; shield or shade sources out of the field of view; diffuse a harsh point source; tilt the part when you can; and control bright backgrounds behind the work. Seconds of repositioning recover more contrast than watts ever will.

sections.caption: Reflected glare happens when the eye sits at the mirror angle; moving the lamp or your head off that angle restores contrast at the surface.
The three glare types and the first fix for each.
headers: What it looks like on the job
rows: Floodlight, sun, or a welding arc sitting in your field of view behind the work
Shield the source or change your line of sight
Hot stripe of lamplight flashing off a shiny surface straight into your eye
Move the lamp or your head off the mirror angle
No dazzle — just washed-out, low-contrast viewing whenever a bright source is near
Keep stray bright light out of the eye; shade and reposition

sections.heading: Fatigue: The Slow Leak in Detection
sections.body:
Visual fatigue is a lighting problem before it is a stamina problem. Symptoms build quietly: stinging or dry eyes, brow-ache and headache, blur that takes longer to clear when you refocus, and a miss rate that climbs toward the end of a shift. The indication does not change — your detector does.

The usual drivers are all manageable. Glare forces constant re-adaptation. Flicker — old magnetic-ballast fluorescents, cheaply dimmed LEDs — hammers the visual system even when you cannot consciously see it. Low contrast makes the eye hunt and strain. Awkward posture and viewing angles add muscular load to ocular load, and walking repeatedly between bright daylight and a dark vessel interior forces adaptation swings that cost both time and sensitivity.

Countermeasures: meet the illuminance minimums, kill the flicker and the glare, take short visual breaks during long examinations, rotate the most demanding close-visual tasks across the crew when the schedule allows, and treat persistent eyestrain as an equipment fault to be fixed — find the bad lamp, ballast, or geometry and correct it.

sections.heading: General Lighting Requirements on Real Jobs
sections.body:
Pull the threads together and the general requirements read like a short pre-job checklist.

- **Know the number.** The procedure states minimum illuminance for general and critical examination and how to verify it. If it is silent, get direction in writing — do not improvise.
- **Layer the light.** Ambient lighting to move and work safely, plus a task light you control for the examination itself. One never substitutes for the other.
- **Verify and document.** Calibrated meter, sensor at the surface, working lamp at working distance, worst-case location; record reading, meter, lamp, and date.
- **Manage quality, not just quantity.** Position for zero specular glare into the eye, reasonable uniformity, and shadows only where you put them deliberately — raking light for relief is a technique; random shadows are a hazard.
- **Respect electrical and atmosphere safety.** Low-voltage or intrinsically safe lighting in vessels and wet work, ground-fault protection on portable cords, hot halogen housings away from solvents and coatings.
- **Maintain the kit.** Fresh batteries, clean lenses, spare lamps — a dimming flashlight fails a lux check long before it fails to switch on.

Lighting set up this way is repeatable: any qualified inspector gets the same view.

figures.title: Verifying Illuminance at the Examination Surface

checkQuestions.question: For a critical or direct visual examination, common code practice requires verifying a minimum illuminance at the surface of about:
checkQuestions.explanation: Roughly 1000 lx (100 fc) is the widely used minimum for critical or direct examination; about 500 lx (50 fc) is the common general-examination floor. The governing procedure always rules.

checkQuestions.question: During illuminance verification, the light-meter sensor belongs:
options: At your eye, aimed back at the lamp
In the plane of the examination surface, at the actual location to be examined, with the working lamp at working distance
One meter from the surface, facing the open room
Anywhere in the work area, as long as the lamp is switched on
checkQuestions.explanation: The requirement applies at the surface being examined, so the sensor lies in that surface's plane at the real examination spot — measured with the actual lamp at the actual distance and aim.

checkQuestions.question: A bright stripe of lamplight reflects off a polished flange face directly into your eye. The most effective immediate fix is to:
options: Move the lamp or your viewing position off the specular (mirror) angle
checkQuestions.explanation: Reflected glare follows the law of reflection — angle in equals angle out. Breaking that lamp-surface-eye alignment removes the hot spot; more wattage only makes it worse.

checkQuestions.question: Your procedure requires 50 fc and your meter reads in lux. The minimum acceptable reading is about:
checkQuestions.explanation: One footcandle is about 10.76 lux, so 50 fc × 10.76 ≈ 538 lx — call it 540 lx on the meter.

checkQuestions.question: Which combination most accelerates visual fatigue on a long shift?
options: Diffuse, steady lighting just above the procedure minimum
Flickering ballasts, glare near the line of sight, and low-contrast surfaces
Short visual breaks between examination areas
checkQuestions.explanation: Flicker, glare, and low contrast each force the visual system to work harder; stacked together they drive eyestrain, headache, and a rising miss rate toward the end of the shift.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, VT Level II Topical Outline §3.3–3.5
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 (illuminance minimums and direct-viewing geometry, cited generically)
SNT-TC-1A and NAS410 employer written practice (vision, documentation, and qualification context)

simulator.title: Lighting Trainer: Make the Surface Readable
simulator.intro: This steel surface contains indications you cannot see at ambient shop light. Raise the illumination, watch the lux meter, and notice exactly when the surface becomes readable — then find what was hiding.
